coredata.dc:title: Sensitive immunoassay of von Willebrand factor based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer between graphene quantum dots and Ag@Au nanoparticles
coredata.dc:creator: Kong, Liyan
Li, Yi
Ma, Chao
Liu, Baihui
Tan, Liang
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and core-shell Ag@Au nanoparticles (Ag@Au NPs) were synthetized and they were characterized by transmission electron microscope and X-ray photoelectron spectra, respectively. Von Willebrand factor antibody (vWF Ab) was bound on Ag@Au NPs to construct Ag@Au-Ab nanocomposites (Ag@Au-Ab NCs). The fluorescence of GQDs could be effectively quenched by the prepared nanocomposites owing to fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). The immunoreaction between vWF and Ag@Au-Ab NCs resulted in the declined FRET efficiency and a degree of fluorescence recovery of GQDs. The fluorescence intensity change was found to be proportional to the logarithm of the vWF concentration in the range of 0.1 pg mL−1–10 ng mL−1 with a detection limit of 30 fg mL−1. The proposed fluorescence sensor was employed to investigate the relationship between the release of vWF and the oxidation-injury degree of vascular endothelial cells. The experimental results indicate that the vWF content in the growth medium was enhanced and the cell injury was intensified when the contact time of the cells with H2O2 was increased.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Ag@Au nanoparticles
Fluorescence resonance energy transfer
von Willebrand factor
Immunoassay

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of GQDs and Ag@Au NPs
FRET between GQDs and Ag@Au-Ab NCs and the negative effect of vWF
Investigation on the release of vWF by oxidation-injured endothelial cells

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Opening Fund of Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Traditional Chinese Medicine Research
State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics
Ministry of Education of the People's Republic of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Open Sustentation Fund of State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics, Hunan University (2017004) and the Opening Fund of Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education of China), Hunan Normal University (KLCBTCMR201705).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Upper part: TEM image of Ag@Au NPs. Lower part: Ag 3d and Au 4f regions in XPS of Ag@Au NPs.

Fluorescence excitation (A) and emission (B) spectra of GQDs in pH 7.5 PBS in the absence (a, a') and presence of Ag@Au NPs (b, b'), Ag@Au-Ab NCs (c, c') and the mixture comprising Ag@Au-Ab NCs and 5 ng mL−1 vWF (d, d'). Excitation slit: 10 nm, emission slit: 10 nm. (C) Fluorescence emission spectrum of GQDs (e) and absorption spectrum of Ag@Au NPs (f).

Schematic illustration for the detection of vWF based on FRET. (A) Strong fluorescence of GQDs. (B) Fluorescence quenching due to FRET between GQDs and Ag@Au NPs. (C) Enhanced fluorescence due to the increased distance between GQDs and Ag@Au-Ab NCs. (D) Recovery of fluorescence owing to the binding of vWF with Ag@Au-Ab NCs.

(A-B) The effects of pH (A) and the incubation time of vWF with Ag@Au-Ab NCs (B) on the difference between the fluorescence intensity of GQDs in the presence of Ag@Au-Ab NCs and that in the presence of the mixture comprising Ag@Au-Ab NCs and 10 ng mL−1 vWF (ΔF). Results are presented as mean ± SD (error bar) of triplicate experiments. (C) Fluorescence emission spectra of GQDs in pH 7.5 PBS in the presence of the mixture comprising Ag@Au-Ab NCs and vWF with different concentrations. The concentration of vWF (from a to h): 0, 1 × 10−4, 1 × 10−3, 1 × 10−2, 0.1, 1, 5 and 10 ng mL−1. Insert: Plots of the fluorescence intensity change (ΔF) vs. the logarithm of the vWF concentration (lgc vWF). Results are presented as mean ± SD (error bar) of triplicate experiments.

(A-E) Microscope images (400×) of HUVEC-C cells in growth phase before (A) and after (B-E) the addition of 1 mM H2O2. The contact time of HUVEC-C with H2O2: 2 h (B), 8 h (C), 16 h (D) and 24 h (E). (F) The relationship between the fluorescence intensity difference induced by vWF in growth medium (ΔF vWF) and the contact time of HUVEC-C with H2O2. Results are presented as mean ± SD (error bar) of triplicate experiments.

head.ce:title: Sensitive immunoassay of von Willebrand factor based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer between graphene quantum dots and Ag@Au nanoparticles

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education of China), College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan Normal University, Changsha, 410081, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education of China)
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this article.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and core-shell Ag@Au nanoparticles (Ag@Au NPs) were synthetized and they were characterized by transmission electron microscope and X-ray photoelectron spectra, respectively. Von Willebrand factor antibody (vWF Ab) was bound on Ag@Au NPs to construct Ag@Au-Ab nanocomposites (Ag@Au-Ab NCs). The fluorescence of GQDs could be effectively quenched by the prepared nanocomposites owing to fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). The immunoreaction between vWF and Ag@Au-Ab NCs resulted in the declined FRET efficiency and a degree of fluorescence recovery of GQDs. The fluorescence intensity change was found to be proportional to the logarithm of the vWF concentration in the range of 0.1 pg mL−1–10 ng mL−1 with a detection limit of 30 fg mL−1. The proposed fluorescence sensor was employed to investigate the relationship between the release of vWF and the oxidation-injury degree of vascular endothelial cells. The experimental results indicate that the vWF content in the growth medium was enhanced and the cell injury was intensified when the contact time of the cells with H2O2 was increased.

ce:section.ce:para: Von Willebrand factor (vWF) is a multimeric plasma glycoprotein comprising a variable number of identical subunits that are composed of 2050 amino acid residues and up to 22 carbohydrate side chains [1]. It originates from the precursor pro-von Willebrand factor and it is synthesized exclusively by endothelial cells and megakaryocytes. This protein exhibits some important biological functions, for example, mediating platelet adhesion and aggregation as well as binding and transporting the pro-coagulant factor VIII (FVIII) [2]. Nonexistence, deficiency and functional ineffectiveness of vWF are found to be involved in von Willebrand disease (vWD), a severe bleeding disorder in which blood cannot clot properly [3]. Recent investigations indicate that vWF can also play a role in regulating angiogenesis, smooth muscle cell proliferation, tumor cell metastasis and crosstalk in the immune system [4,5].

Vascular endothelium is located at the interface between tissue and blood and it consists of a single layer of polygonal cells that are named as endothelial cells (ECs). Endothelium plays a key part in regulating some physiologic and pathophysiologic processes including hemostasis, thrombosis, inflammation and immune responses [6]. The balance between hemostasis and thrombosis is maintained by some procoagulation and anticoagulation proteins that are released by ECs [3]. When ECs suffer from activation and injury, the coordinate induction of procoagulant/prothrombotic factors and suppression of anticoagulant mechanisms may break this balance and facilitate platelet aggregation and clot formation [7]. It is reported that vWF, thrombomodulin and tissue factor pathway inhibitor are the three major hemostatic regulatory molecules [8] and the levels of the first two in plasma are increased significantly with endothelial activation and injury [9,10]. Therefore, vWF is regarded as a promising biomarker of ECs dysfunction.

Carbon-based nanomaterials including carbon nanotubes, carbon dots and graphene have received considerable attentions among analysts and they are widely employed in various chem/biosensors. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), which are actually graphene fragments with size less than 20 nm, exhibit an excellent solubility, low toxicity and biocompatibility [11,12]. The specific photoluminescence performances of GQDs, for example, longer fluorescence life time, better photo-stability and higher quantum yield in comparison with conventional dye molecules and semiconductor quantum dots, have inspired extensive researches in fluorescence measurements [13–15]. Some metal nanoparticles, such as gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) and silver nanoparticles (Ag NPs), display high extinction coefficients and distance-dependent optical properties in the visible-region [16]. As a kind of bimetallic nanomaterial, core-shell nanoparticles not only combine properties of individual metallic elements but also exhibit more excellent features than individual component, such as enhanced stability and dispersion, improved biocompatibility and promoted catalytic activity [17,18]. During the past years, some optical or electrochemical sensors empolying silver@gold nanoparticles (Ag@Au NPs) for the detection of carcinoembryonic antigen [19], Hg2+ [20], Cd2+ [21], cyanide [22], biothiols [23], L-methionine [18], and so on, have been developed.

Fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) occurs between a donor molecule in the excited state and an acceptor molecule in the ground state. The donor molecules typically emit at shorter wavelengths that overlap with the absorption spectrum of the acceptor [24]. It is a sensitive and reliable technique in fluorescence analysis. The fluorescence emission spectrum of GQDs is usually presented in visible-region and it can overlap with the absorption spectra of many acceptors to a certain extent. Some FRET sensors using GQDs as the donor fluorophore have been reported [25–30]. To our knowledge, to date there are no reports on the fluorescent detection of vWF using GQDs and Ag@Au NPs as the donor and the acceptor, separately. In the present work, GQDs and Ag@Au NPs were synthetized successfully and the fluorescence quenching of the former by the latter due to FRET was achieved. The Ag@Au-antibody nanocomposites were prepared by using vWF antibody, bovine serum albumin and the core-shell nanoparticles. Highly sensitive and selective fluorescent detection of vWF was realized based on the negative effect of the vWF-nanocomposites binding on FRET. In addition, the proposed method was employed to investigate the release of vWF by the oxidation-injured vascular endothelial cells.

vWF protein and rabbit anti-human vWF antibody (vWF Ab) were purchased from Beijing Biosynthesis Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (China). Bovine serum albumin (BSA, Sigma-Aldrich) was used as received. Citric acid (CA), sodium citrate, sodium alginate, HAuCl4, AgNO3, NH2OH·HCl, NaBH4 and NaOH were purchased from Xiya Chemical Industry Co., Ltd. (Linshu, China). The buffer for the assay was 0.1 M phosphate buffer saline (PBS), prepared by mixing stock standard solution of Na2HPO4 and KH2PO4. All aqueous solutions were prepared in Milli-Q ultrapure water.

All fluorescence measurements were performed on a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer. A UV-2450 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) was employed for the absorption spectrum analysis. The sizes of GQDs and Ag@Au NPs were characterized ex situ by a Tecnai G2 20ST transmission electron microscope (TEM, FEI, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were recorded on an ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, UK). The cell-modality observation was carried out with an Eclipse Ti-S inverted fluorescence microscope (Nikon, Japan) using a × 10 eyepiece and a × 40 objective lens in bright-field mode.

GQDs were prepared by the modified CA pyrolysis method [31]. Firstly, 0.5 g CA was heated to 175 °C in an oil bath until liquidated. Subsequently, it was further heated for 30 min until the color of the liquid changed from colorless to orange-red. The liquid was added to 50 mL 1% NaOH solution slowly under vigorous stirring and then it was sonicated for 30 min for complete dissolution. Finally, the prepared solution was dialyzed by using a 300 D dialysis bag until the external solution became neutral. The obtained yellow GQDs solution was stored at 4 °C when not used.

Ag@Au NPs were synthesized using silver as the core and gold as the shell [22,23]. Firstly, 250 μL of 0.1 M AgNO3 and 250 μL of 0.1 M trisodium citrate were added into 100 mL ultrapure water under stirring. Subsequently, 6 mL of freshly prepared 25 mM NaBH4 solution was added quickly into this mixture and the solution was stirred vigorously at room temperature for 30 min until its color changed from colorless to yellow, which meant the formation silver nanoparticles (Ag NPs). Next, 10 mL of 0.465 mM HAuCl4 and 10 mL of 6.25 mM NH2OH·HCl were separately dropped into the mixture containing 12.5 mL of the as-obtained colloidal Ag NPs and 10 mL ultrapure water. Finally, the mixed solution was stirred for another 45 min until its color became wine red, suggesting that Ag@Au NPs had formed.

The preparation of Ag@Au-Ab nanocomposites (Ag@Au-Ab NCs) was performed as following: while gently stirring, 1 mL of 50 mg mL−1 vWF was slowly added into 10 mL of Ag@Au NPs suspension and then the mixture was continuously stirred for 4 h at 4 °C. Next, 200 μL of 1% BSA solution was added to cover the nonspecific sites on the surface of Ag@Au-Ab NCs. After 4-h stirring, the solution was centrifuged at 13,000 rpm for 30 min. The precipitate was re-dispersed in 5 mL of pH 7.4 PBS containing 0.1% sodium alginate. The such-made composite suspension could be stored at 4 °C for more than 1 month without a significant decrease in affinity for vWF proteins.

All experiments, except where specified, were carried out at pH 7.5 PBS. 100 μL Ag@Au-Ab NCs and 20 μL vWF with different concentration were added into a 0.5-mL polyethylene centrifuge tube and the mixture was incubated for 150 min at ambient temperature. Then, 20 μL GQDs suspension solution was added to the above solution. The final volume of mixture was adjusted to 300 μL with buffer solution. The mixture was subjected to the fluorescence wavelength scan using a 300-μL micro quartz cell (1 cm path length) with the following instrumental parameters: excitation wavelength (λ ex), 361 nm; excitation slit (EX), 10 nm; emission wavelength range (λ em), 410–650 nm; emission slit (EM), 10 nm.

Human umbilical vein endothelial cell line, HUVEC-C, was obtained from Xiangya School of Medicine, Central South University (China) and cultured at 37 °C with 5% CO2 using DMEM/low glucose growth medium (HyClone) supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS). The trypsinized and re-suspended cells were seeded in some wells on a 96-well plate at the concentration 5×104 cells mL−1. Twelve hours later, HUVEC-C cells spread over the substrate. Next, 10 μL of H2O2 was added into the wells and the cells were continuously cultured for several hours. Finally, 20 μL of growth medium was transferred into 100 μL of Ag@Au-Ab NCs suspension by using a micropipettor. The mixture was added into pH 7.5 PBS containing GQDs. The fluorescence emission spectra were collected based on the same instrumental parameters for the detection of vWF.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of GQDs and Ag@Au NPs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphologies of GQDs and Ag@Au NPs were characterized by TEM. Fig. 1 shows that lots of GQDs were well-dispersed and they exhibited nearly spherical shape with diameter about 5 nm. The shape and size of the prepared GQDs were similar to those reported in the references [26,28,30]. As shown in Fig. 2 , Ag@Au NPs displayed contrast features with a bright core and a dark shell. These nanoparticles’ diameter was measured to be around 16 nm. The XPS analysis was performed to investigate the composition of Ag@Au NPs and the result is shown in Fig. 2. The resolved Au 4f signals, Au 4f7/2 at 84.0 eV and Au 4f5/2 at 87.6 eV, could be observed. They were ascribed to the existence of metallic gold (Au0) [32]. In addition, the Ag 3d spectra of the core-shell nanoparticles consisted of two individual peaks at 374 and 368 eV, corresponding to Ag 3d3/2 and Ag 3d5/2 binding energies, respectively, which were indicative of the presence of metallic silver (Ag0) [33].

ce:section.ce:section-title: FRET between GQDs and Ag@Au-Ab NCs and the negative effect of vWF
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3 A and B show the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of GQDs in pH 7.5 PBS in the absence and presence of Ag@Au NPs, Ag@Au-Ab NCs and the mixture comprising Ag@Au-Ab NPs and vWF, respectively. One can find the maximum fluorescence excitation and emission intensity of GQDs at 361 nm and 458 nm, separately (curves a and a' in Fig. 3). The fluorescence intensities significantly declined in the presence of Ag@Au NPs (curves b and b' in Fig. 3). The UV–vis absorption spectra of Ag@Au NPs and GQDs were measured and the results are shown in Fig. S1. Ag@Au NPs presented a prominent peak at about 503 nm (curve a in Fig. S1), which exhibited a blue-shift in comparison with the characteristic absorbance peak of Au NPs at 523 nm [34]. Two weak absorption peaks at 360 nm and 519 nm were observed on the spectra of GQDs (curve b in Fig. S1). The spectrum of the mixture containing Ag@Au NPs and GQDs was recorded as curve c in Fig. S1, on which two absorption peaks at 375 nm and 505 nm could be found. In addition, the sum of the absorbance of Ag@Au NPs and that of GQDs at the same wavelength was obtained and it was displayed as curve d in Fig. S1. The absorbance on curve c was inferior to that on curve d, suggesting that the weak interaction existed between Ag@Au NPs and GQDs. Fig. 3C shows that the emission spectrum of GQDs partly overlapped with the absorption spectrum of Ag@Au NPs. It means that GQDs and Ag@Au NPs might act as the donor and the acceptor for fluorescence resonance energy transfer, respectively. It can be deduced from the above investigation that FRET between the two nanomaterials caused the effective fluorescence quenching of GQDs. The relevant reaction process and result are shown in Fig. 4 A and B. Curves c and c' in Fig. 3 indicate that the fluorescence-quenching extent of GQDs in the presence of Ag@Au-Ab NCs was inferior to that in the presence of Ag@Au NPs. As shown in Fig. 4C, the immobilization of vWF Ab and BSA on Ag@Au NPs might lead to the steric hindrance effect that could increase the distance between GQDs and Ag@Au NPs and weaken the FRET efficiency. It is found from curves d and d' in Fig. 3 that the fluorescence of GQDs system was further recovered/turned on in the presence of Ag@Au-Ab NCs and vWF. This result indicates that the immunoreaction of vWF proteins with the antibody molecules on the nanocomposites had a seriously negative influence on the above-mentioned FRET. Fig. 4D shows that the fluorescence of GQDs re-appeared when these fluorescence molecules were effectively separated from the nanocomposite.

In this work, the detection of vWF was performed based on the recovery of the GQDs fluorescence induced by the immunoreaction of vWF. Two factors including pH and the incubation time of vWF with Ag@Au-Ab NCs, which might affect the immunoreaction, were investigated. The fluorescence intensity of GQDs in the presence of Ag@Au-Ab NCs (F 1) and that in the presence of the mixture comprising Ag@Au-Ab NCs and vWF (F 2) were measured, separately, and their difference, ΔF (ΔF = F 2-F 1) was calculated. The effects of pH and the incubation time on the ΔF value are shown in Fig. 5 A and B, respectively. Panel A displays that the optimal fluorescence intensity difference was achieved at pH 7.5 and acidic or alkaline conditions all led to the declined ΔF values. It is reported that the bioactivity of the immobilized protein is solution pH dependent [35] and the isoelectric point of vWF protein is around pH 5.45 [36]. Acidic medium might decrease the bioactivity of antibody/antigen. The electrostatic repulsion would exist between negatively charged vWF molecules and biomolecules immobilized on Ag@Au-Ab NCs in the alkaline solution. As a result, the immunoreaction was negatively affected in acidic or alkaline medium and the separation of GQDs from Ag@Au-Ab NCs could not be effectively realized. So PBS at pH 7.5 was selected for all the subsequent experiments. Panel B shows that the ΔF value rapidly increased with the increasing incubation time of vWF from 0.5 to 2.5 h and the longer incubation time (3.0 h) could not improve the ΔF response. Therefore, 2.5 h was chosen as the incubation time for the vWF determination since the capture of vWF by Ag@Au-Ab NCs required enough time.

Under optimum conditions, the fluorescence emission spectra of GQDs in the presence of the mixture comprising Ag@Au-Ab NCs and vWF with different concentrations were measured and the results are shown in Fig. 5C. The fluorescence intensity was found to be enhanced in sequence when the vWF concentration was increased, which means that GQDs were gradually separated from Ag@Au-Ab NCs due to the binding of vWF with the gold nanocomposites. The insert shows that the ΔF value was proportional to the logarithm of the vWF concentration from 0.1 pg mL−1 to 10 ng mL−1. The regression equation was ΔF = 62.5lgc vWF + 297 with a high correlation coefficient of 0.995 and the limit of detection (LOD) was 30 fg mL−1 estimated at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3. The coefficient of variation (CV) for the ΔF value at the different vWF concentrations are shown in Tables S1. They were found to be less than 15%, which is the most commonly used CV cut-off value [37]. Hence, the highly sensitive quantitative detection of vWF could be realized by using the proposed turn-on fluorescence method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation on the release of vWF by oxidation-injured endothelial cells
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of the developed fluorescence immunosensor was investigated. Some co-existing components in DMEM growth medium supplemented with 10% FBS, for example, BSA, glucose, serine, glycine, riboflavin and folic acid, as well as thrombomodulin, which might be released by injured vascular endothelial cells [34], were evaluated, respectively. The fluorescence intensity of GQDs in the presence of Ag@Au-Ab NCs and that in the presence of the mixture comprising Ag@Au-Ab NCs and 10 ng mL−1 vWF or the above interfering components with 10-fold concentration was detected, separately, and their difference was obtained. Fig. S2 shows that the ΔF value caused by vWF was much larger than that derived from these interfering components. It means a specific reaction between vWF molecules and Ag@Au-Ab NCs. In other words, the proposed FRET immunosensor could selectively detect vWF in growth medium.

In this work, the oxidation injury of vascular endothelial cells was actualized by H2O2. This strong oxidizer with the final concentration of 1 mM was introduced into the cell-culture wells when HUVEC-C cells were in their growth phase. Fig. 6 A displays the microscope image of HUVEC-C cells in the absence of H2O2. It is found that the cells adhered on the substrate were closely interconnected as a monolayer, exhibiting irregular polygon shape and distinct cellular outline. The microscopic photos of the same cells at 2 h, 8 h, 16 h and 24 h after the addition of H2O2 are shown in Fig. 6B–E, respectively. After two-hour stimulation of H2O2, the obvious shape change of the cells was observed. HUVEC-C cells began to shrink and the cell–cell separation appeared (panel B). The spreading area of these cells was further decreased with the increase of contact time of the cells with H2O2. HUVEC-C cells generated apparent protuberances on their surfaces at 8 h when almost all the cells were separated from each other (panel C). Subsequently, the cells gradually expanded and finally became circle-like vesicles exhibiting vague cellular outline at 16 h (panel D). Twenty four hours later, the cells broke into pieces and released much cell debris. As a result, no living cells were located on the substrate (panel E). It is reported that active oxygen species (ROS) including H2O2 are able to induce cell injury and lysis in various target cells both in vivo and in vitro [38]. They may break DNA, cleave polysaccharides, inactivate enzymes, oxidize proteins, produce lipid peroxidation and cause chromosomal alterations [39,40]. Our cell-observation results support this conclusion that H2O2 led to the serious oxidation injury of HUVEC-C cells.

The relationship between the vWF release and the oxidation injury of HUVEC-C cells was analyzed in the present work. The growth media in the five cell incubation wells, in which HUVEC-C cells exhibited the five different states shown in Fig. 6A–E, i.e. the cells in the growth phase and the injured cells at 2 h, 8 h, 16 h and 24 h, were employed and marked as GM0, GM2, GM8, GM16 and GM24, respectively. The fluorescence intensity of GQDs in pH 7.5 PBS in the presence of the mixture comprising Ag@Au-Ab NCs and these five growth media was measured and they were recorded as F 0, F 2, F 8, F 16 and F 24, separately. The five F values were found to increase in sequence. The difference between the first (F 0) and the latter four (F 2-F 24) was calculated, respectively, and the result was defined as ΔF vWF. Fig. 6F shows the ΔF vWF value corresponding to the different contact time of HUVEC-C with H2O2 was linear with the contact time in the range from 2 to 24 h with a correlation coefficient of 0.994. The CV values for the fluorescence-intensity change are shown in Tables S2 and they were also less than 15%. One can conclude from Fig. 6 that the uninterrupted stimulation of H2O2 could intensify the injury of HUVEC-C cells and result in the gradual release of vWF. More and more vWF molecules in growth medium reacted with Ag@Au-Ab NCs, exerting the increasingly negative influence on FRET between GQDs and the gold nanocomposites due to the steric hindrance effect. As a result, the fluorescence intensity of GQDs was continuously recovered.

GQDs prepared by CA pyrolysis method displayed nearly spherical shape with diameter about 5 nm and exhibited the excellent fluorescent performance. Ag@Au NPs synthesized based on a two-step method presented the obvious core-shell structure. The fluorescence of GQDs was effectively quenched by Ag@Au NPs and Ag@Au-Ab NCs due to FRET. The immunoreaction between vWF molecules and their antibodies on Ag@Au-Ab NCs generated the steric hindrance effect and weakened the FRET efficiency, leading to some recovery of the GQDs fluorescence. The sensitive and selective detection of vWF was carried out by employing the proposed fluorescence method. H2O2 was selected to induce the oxidation injury of HUVEC-C cells. The injury extent was found to be intensified and vWF was gradually released by the vascular endothelial cells under the continuous stimulation of H2O2. The proposed FRET biosensor using GQDs and Ag@Au NPs as the donor and the acceptor, respectively, may find its potential application in immunoassay if antibodies immobilized on Ag@Au NPs can maintain high activity and effectively capture antigen molecules.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Open Sustentation Fund of State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics, Hunan University (2017004) and the Opening Fund of Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education of China), Hunan Normal University (KLCBTCMR201705).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.colsurfb.2018.02.049.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
